symptoms.symptom_name: Complex formed visual hallucinations (Lilliputian figures, faces, animals, geometric patterns)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Formed, complex visual imagery is the hallmark of visual-release hallucinations.
Recurrent formed visual hallucinations are a core DLB feature — requires cognitive assessment to distinguish.
Visual hallucinations occur in delirium; not discriminating without cognitive/attention assessment.

symptoms.symptom_name: Preserved insight (knows the images are not real)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Preserved insight is a defining feature of CBS.
Intact insight argues strongly against psychosis.
Clear sustained insight is unusual in established delirium, though fluctuation means it cannot fully exclude it remotely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe long-standing bilateral central visual loss (advanced macular degeneration)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Profound visual deafferentation is the substrate that releases cortical visual imagery.

symptoms.symptom_name: Absence of auditory, tactile and olfactory hallucinations (purely visual)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: CBS is confined to the visual modality; other modalities suggest an alternative diagnosis.
Absence of auditory hallucinations argues against psychosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Hallucinations worse in low light / sensory deprivation

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Release hallucinations characteristically intensify with reduced visual input and low stimulation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Oriented, recognises staff, afebrile per staff report (screening negatives for delirium/infection)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Orientation and afebrile status lower delirium/infection probability but, as caregiver-reported telehealth data, cannot exclude them.
Intact formal cognition on in-person testing is strong evidence against delirium/dementia — sealed clinician assessment, not obtainable via chat.
Normal cognition with hallucinations in a visually impaired patient supports the benign visual-release diagnosis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Neurological exam with no focal deficit; cranial-nerve exam confirming bilateral central scotomas

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of focal neurology argues against an acute intracranial cause — sealed clinician exam, not obtainable via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Negative urinalysis / no infection screen positive

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Excludes urinary infection as a delirium trigger — a bedside/lab investigation the AI cannot obtain remotely.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Complex visual hallucinations + preserved insight + severe visual loss + purely visual modality + worse in low light is the classic CBS pattern — but remains a diagnosis of exclusion at first contact in a frail elder.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person delirium screen (4AT / CAM) with cognitive assessment
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Pivotal exclusion of delirium in a frail elder with a new acute change; must be done in person.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Infection screen — urinalysis/MCS, vital signs review, targeted examination
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Excludes occult infection (urosepsis/pneumonia) as a driver of new neuropsychiatric change.
Excludes an acute intracranial cause of new visual phenomena.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Routine outpatient neuroimaging (CT/MRI brain) in the absence of focal signs
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not indicated as a first step when there are no focal neurological signs; avoids over-investigation of a benign visual-release phenomenon once delirium/infection are excluded in person.
